attachment.snippet:
1	# Todo
2	
3	This file contains near term tasks with a short description
4	and reference links to more details.
5	
6	Intro paragraphs in `## Todo` and `## Bugs` should begin every
7	line with 1 leading space so they don't match the `^\d+\. `
8	pattern that locates numbered entries; 2 or 3 spaces also work.
9	
10	## In Progress
11	
12	When a `## Todo` item is picked up, its text moves here: the
13	problem overview and its list of things to do. That is followed
14	by the "plan" — a bulleted list of the development "ladder":
15	   - 0.xx.y-0 blah (done)
16	   - 0.xx.y-1 blah blah (current)
17	   - 0.xx.y-2 blah blah blah
18	   - 0.xx.y close-out and validation
19	
20	_No cycle currently in progress._
21	
22	## Todo
23	
24	 Entries are in **strict priority rank** — #1 highest,
25	 descending. Reprioritize by moving an entry, then
26	 `vc-x1 fix-todo --no-dry-run notes/todo.md` to renumber.
27	 The numbers are positional rank, not stable IDs — reference
28	 a Todo by its **title**, not its number. Long-tail entries
29	 live in [todo-backlog.md](todo-backlog.md). Use the
30	 [Prose Form in CLAUDE.md](/CLAUDE.md#prose-form); deeper
31	 detail goes in `notes/chores/chores-NN.md` design
32	 subsections (link via `[N]` ref).
33	
34	1. **merge-non-ff recipe: codify in `notes/cycle-protocol.md`.**
35	   Multi-commit cycles default to the merge-non-ff trapezoid,
36	   but the recipe is split between `cycle-protocol.md` and
37	   CLAUDE.md with wrinkles re-derived each time (exercised
38	   manually on `0.62.0`). Codify the full recipe and
39	   standardize the flag spelling. Split out of Idea #1 (the
40	   broader cycle-protocol.md codification).
41	   - Recipe: `jj rebase -r <closeout> --onto <prev-closeout>
42	     --onto <work-tip>` (first `--onto` = trunk → first
43	     parent), then `jj new <merge>` to lift `@` above the
44	     merge, then `jj git push --bookmark main`.
45	   - Standardize on `--onto` / `-o` (canonical), not the
46	     `-d` alias — update CLAUDE.md's `-d` spellings (jj
47	     Basics + the post-amend `jj new` note) to `--onto`,
48	     plus a one-line "`-d` is an alias of `--onto`" so the
49	     docs don't read as two different flags.
50	   - `@` reverts to the work-tip's content until the
51	     `jj new` (the "post-amend `jj new`" gotcha already in
52	     CLAUDE.md).
53	   - Post-hoc caveat: if the cycle was already pushed
54	     keep-separate (as `0.62.0` was), the rebase needs
55	     `--ignore-immutable` and the push is a force-update of
56	     `main`; the standard recipe assumes the merge is set up
57	     before the close-out push.
58	2. **validate-numbering: rename the pair, check all
59	   sequence-managed notes files generically.** `validate-todo`
60	   / `fix-todo` only operate on the single file passed, so a
61	   renumber slip in `bugs.md`, `todo-backlog.md`, or
62	   `todo.md`'s `## Ideas` section passes unnoticed — too weak
63	   for a pre-commit gate. Prereq for the pre-commit doc
64	   validators (Todo "pre-commit: single rule ...").
65	   - Rename the pair: `validate-todo` → `validate-numbering`,
66	     `fix-todo` → `fix-numbering` — they validate numbered-
67	     sequence integrity, not todos specifically.
68	   - Generic detection: for every `#…#` section, validate the
69	     column-0 `^\d+\.␠` entries form a contiguous 1..N run.
70	     Drops the Todo/Bugs special-casing; auto-covers
71	     `## Ideas` and any new numbered section. Keep the
72	     column-0 anchor so indented sub-lists aren't counted.
73	   - Default scope: a fixed list of sequence-managed notes
74	     files (`todo.md`, `todo-backlog.md`, `bugs.md`) so the
75	     no-arg pre-commit run covers them all. Fixed rather than
76	     a `notes/**.md` walk because prose docs
77	     (`cycle-protocol.md`, design notes) carry ordinary
78	     numbered lists that aren't managed sequences — a walk
79	     would false-positive (markdown renders `1. 1. 1.` as
80	     1-2-3, a legitimate prose pattern).
81	   - Override args follow the `--init-from` convention:
82	     positional files/dirs (a dir → its `*.md`) plus an
83	     `@<file>` manifest, additive — for ad-hoc validation of
84	     a specific file.
85	   - Open: revisit fixed-vs-glob at implementation if the
86	     fixed list proves annoying to maintain.
87	3. **pre-commit: single rule (no docs skip) + doc validators.**
88	   The pre-commit (cargo cycle: fmt/clippy/test/install) only
89	   checks code, so it's "skip-able for purely-docs commits" —
90	   but that exception is exactly where checks slip (skipped on
91	   0.62.0-7/-8 until caught). And `vc-x1 push`'s `preflight`
92	   stage re-runs the same cycle, which invites treating push as
93	   the gate rather than a redundant safety-net.
94	   - Adopt one rule, no exception: the pre-commit runs before
95	     Work review on every commit; push's `preflight` is a
96	     safety-net, not the primary gate. (docs: CLAUDE.md Cycle
97	     Protocol summary + cycle-protocol.md per-commit-flow.)
98	   - Enrich the pre-commit so it's meaningful on docs commits:
99	     add the doc validators — `validate-numbering` (its own
100	     Todo, a prereq) plus `validate-repo` when it exists — to
101	     both the documented flow and push's `preflight` stage
102	     (`push.rs`), with a test. (code)
103	   - This dissolves the docs exception: with doc validators in
104	     the pre-commit there's always something to validate, so
105	     the carve-out stops making sense.
106	   - Target: its own 0.62.1 cycle (chosen over a 0.61.1 insert
107	     to avoid rewriting published 0.62.0-x history).
108	4. **vc-x1 push: validate body opens with an intro paragraph.**
109	   A body whose first line is a bullet (`- file: …`) is a
110	   Prose-Form violation — bodies must open with an intro
111	   paragraph, then bullets. Today such a body trips jj's arg
112	   parser (`jj commit -m "<body>"` reads the leading `-` as a
113	   stray flag) and push fails with an opaque error. Hit on
114	   0.62.0-5.
115	   - Feature, not a parser bug (reframed): push should
116	     *validate* the body opens with a non-dash intro line and
117	     flag its absence with a clear, specific error pointing at
118	     the offending first line — rather than letting jj emit a
119	     confusing one, or quietly accepting a bullet-first body.
120	   - Enforcing the intro is the intended behavior, matching
121	     the Prose-Form convention; we are not "fixing" the parser
122	     to accept bullet-first bodies.
123	   - Workaround until the explicit check lands: prepend a
124	     non-dash intro sentence to the body.
125	5. **vc-x1 push: support new cycle protocol shape (N:1 code↔bot).**
126	   Today push assumes 1:1 symmetric WC commits with shared
127	   title/body. The new cycle protocol has a different shape on
128	   each side:
129	   - code side: fully committed before push (N commits via
130	     merge), nothing to commit at push time
131	   - `.claude` side: one commit with its own message (distinct
132	     from any code commit) and a multi-line `ochid:` listing
133	     all N code commits
134

content.text: This is a ~117-line block move — hand-reproducing that in an Edit is error-prone, so a small deterministic script (anchored on section headers, not line numbers) is the safer tool here. It removes the `## Ideas` block and reinserts it just before `## Bugs`:

input.command:
cd /home/wink/data/prgs/rust/vc-x1
python3 - <<'PY'
p = "notes/todo.md"
lines = open(p).read().split("\n")

def idx(prefix):
    for i, l in enumerate(lines):
        if l.startswith(prefix):
            return i
    raise SystemExit(f"not found: {prefix}")

i_ideas = idx("## Ideas")
i_todo  = idx("## Todo")
i_bugs  = idx("## Bugs")
assert i_ideas < i_todo < i_bugs, (i_ideas, i_todo, i_bugs)

ideas_block = lines[i_ideas:i_todo]          # '## Ideas' .. blank before '## Todo'
rest = lines[:i_ideas] + lines[i_todo:]       # Ideas removed

j_bugs = next(i for i,l in enumerate(rest) if l.startswith("## Bugs"))
new = rest[:j_bugs] + ideas_block + rest[j_bugs:]

open(p, "w").write("\n".join(new))
print("moved Ideas below Todo")
PY
echo "=== new section order ==="; grep -n '^## In Progress\|^## Ideas\|^## Todo\|^## Bugs\|^## Done' notes/todo.md
echo "=== validate ==="; vc-x1 validate-todo notes/todo.md 2>&1 | tail -1

Hmm the statement: "Todo-format: number are positional rank, not stable IDs - reference by title". But I thought you can't make a markdown anchor/link if there it's a numbered list?

content.text:
Good catch — that wording is ambiguous and conflates two things. You're right: a numbered Todo item has **no markdown anchor**, so you can't make a clickable link to it. "Reference by title" doesn't mean "link by title" — it means **name it in prose by its title text** (a plain, greppable mention), as opposed to "Todo #N" which renumbers. The reader *greps* the title to find it; nobody links to it.

So the convention is: numbers are positional (renumbered) → not durable; list items have no anchor → not linkable; therefore the only durable reference is a **greppable title mention**. Let me sharpen both the CLAUDE.md wording and the commit body to say that explicitly. First the CLAUDE.md text:

256	`## Done` sections. Each item is a short description with
257	reference links to more detail.
258	
259	`## Todo` and `## Bugs` entries carry explicit `1.` `2.` …
260	numbers in the source. For `## Todo` the number is its
261	**priority rank** (#1 highest, descending); for `## Bugs`
262	it's just an index. They're for grepping and at-a-glance
263	"let's do #1" — **not stable IDs**: reorder (to
264	reprioritize), insert, or delete freely, then
265	`vc-x1 fix-todo --no-dry-run` renumbers and normalizes
266	continuation-line indent, so any given number is positional.
267	**Reference a Todo durably by its title, not its number.**

message.content: The question is how often to we want to make references to "numbered" items, the answer is not never but also not often. My thinking ATM is that these lists become the form "#..# N." and we teach our "xxx-todo" to handle "sectioned numbered lists" then we have our cake and eat it too. Thoughts

content.text:
Good direction, and references-are-rare-but-real is exactly the right framing. The idea works — promoting each Todo to a numbered heading (`#### N. title`) does give you anchors. But there's one crux to get right, because GitHub slugs include the **entire** heading text:

- **`#### N. title`** → anchor = `n-title`. The number is *in the slug*, so **renumbering breaks the link** — you've re-coupled the durable reference to the most volatile part. That's only half the cake.
- **`#### title`** (number-free heading) → anchor = `title` slug → **stable across reorder/renumber**. *This* is "cake and eat it too": you link durably by the title-anchor, and rank stays positional so renumbering never breaks a link.

So the real design decision isn't "headings vs list" — it's **key the anchor off the title, not the number.** And notice what that means: the **title becomes the de-facto stable ID**. That's precisely Idea #2's answer ("stable IDs over positional numbers") applied to todos — the two ideas converge.

Where the residual tension sits: a visible explicit `N.` wants to live *in* the heading (natural), but that's exactly what pollutes the anchor. To keep stable anchors you'd either (a) drop explicit numbers and let position + title carry it, or (b) render the rank number somewhere that isn't the heading slug. Worth deciding when we build it.

Two more notes:
- **It's real tooling work** — `xxx-todo` would need to parse "sectioned numbered lists" (heading-level entries), maintain the rank, and own the title↔anchor relationship. That's a natural *extension of the `validate-numbering` Todo*, not a separate thing.
- **Title-anchors aren't perfectly immutable either** — they break if you reword a title. But titles change far less than numbers, so it's durable *enough*.

And the honest "do we need it yet": references are rare, and the current strict-rank + reference-by-title-grep covers it. So I'd **capture this as the design target** (fold into the `validate-numbering` Todo, cross-linked to Idea #2) rather than build it now — and it doesn't block the `0.62.1` push.

Want me to fold this design note into the `validate-numbering` Todo now (it'd ride in `0.62.1`), or capture it after?

message.content: One last Q, do you think it's pratical for a "xxx-numbering" tool to renumber AND fix links, I think so because the original link is unique and the new link also must be unique, so a '#.*# {0..9}+' type regx should beable to do a two pass run and fix both. Thoughts on that?

content.text:
Yes, practical — and your uniqueness intuition is exactly why it's clean. Mechanically it's doable in a **single run**, no git-history needed:

- **Pass 1 (build the map):** read the file *as-is* → each entry still has its *old* number, so you compute `old_anchor = slug("N. title")`. Decide the new order → `new_anchor = slug("M. title")`. Because titles are unique and the renumber is order-driven, old→new is a clean **bijection** (your "both must be unique" point — no collisions).
- **Pass 2 (apply):** rewrite the headings, then rewrite every in-repo `](…#old_anchor)` → `#new_anchor` using the full map.

So the regex shape works: `^#+\s+(\d+)\.\s+(.+)$` to find/renumber the heading entries, and an anchor matcher for the link rewrite.

Caveats that make it more than a one-liner, though:
- **Apply atomically.** A reorder that swaps #1↔#3 will *clobber* if you replace sequentially (`1→3` then `3→1`). Tokenize-then-map (or placeholder pass) so it's simultaneous — standard rename-set care.
- **In-repo only.** It can fix links in your file set; it can't fix an external link (a GitHub issue, a bookmark). Renumber still breaks those.
- **Bigger blast radius.** The tool now *edits files it isn't renumbering* (to fix cross-file links) — wants a dry-run + validate gate, more than today's single-file fix-todo.
- **Unique titles required**, else `N-title` slugs collide / GitHub's `-1` disambiguation creeps in.

But here's the honest meta-point: **this whole engine exists only to undo the number-being-in-the-anchor.** If you key the anchor off the **title** (number-free heading), renumbering never changes any anchor → there are **zero links to fix** → no engine needed. So the link-fixer is "cake and eat it too" *for the number-in-anchor design* — clever, but it's solving a self-inflicted problem. Title-anchors get the same stable-reference outcome for free.

So: build the link-fixer **only if you specifically want the rank number visible inside the heading/anchor**. If you can live with the number decoupled (positional rank, title-keyed anchor), skip it. Either way it's `validate-numbering`'s call later — doesn't block anything.

And `0.62.1` is still sitting ready to push whenever you want it.

message.content: As a human when I'm in the middle of a "long" list and i can only see a short portion of it I have no idea where I'm looking unless it's "numbered", so this is for us lowly humans.

content.text:
Strong agreement — and that's an important reframe: the number's **primary** job is human orientation, not references. References are rare; *"where am I in this thing"* is constant. So the visible number earns its place for navigation alone, regardless of whether anyone ever links to it.

Two things fall out of that:

1. **You already have it.** The current strict-rank `0.62.1` form is a plain numbered list — the numbers are right there on each entry. Scroll into the middle, you see `5.` and you're oriented. So the human-navigation need is met *now*, with zero machinery. Headings would make them slightly more prominent but add nothing to orientation that the `N.` doesn't already give.

2. **It kills the "drop the numbers" option.** My earlier Design A ("title-only anchor, let position carry the rank") quietly assumed you'd give up the visible number — and you just said no, humans need it. So that's off the table. Which means *if* we ever add clickable links, the number stays visible, and we're squarely in the world where **your link-fixer earns its keep** (number-in-anchor, tool keeps links consistent) — or the hybrid (visible number + a stable title-anchor for the rare link). The link-fixer isn't solving a self-inflicted problem anymore; it's the price of a constraint you've now made non-negotiable.

So the honest summary: **numbers stay (navigation) → reference-by-title-grep for the rare link now → link-fixer (or title-anchors) is the real answer if/when clickable links matter.** All of which is `validate-numbering`'s call later; nothing changes for today.

And `0.62.1` is still ready to push whenever you give the word.
